title: Atraumatic Care: Nonpharmacologic Comfort for the Hospitalized Child
subtitle: A frightened 4-year-old needs an IV start. The nurse minimizes physical and emotional distress with honest preparation, parental presence, comfort positioning, topical anesthetic, and distraction — using a treatment room and saving the bed and playroom as safe spaces.

patient.name: L. Tran
patient.admitDate: Pediatric unit. A 4-year-old needs an IV start and blood draw. Frightened and clinging to a parent. Nurse applying atraumatic-care principles to reduce distress and pain.

vitals.temp: Parent lap hold + distraction + numbing cream
Held down in bed, no prep -> fear

labs.r: Avoid lying flat and holding down.

nursesNote: L. Tran, 4, needs an IV start and blood draw and is frightened and clinging to her parent. The nurse applies atraumatic care to minimize physical and emotional distress: keep the parent present as a comforter, give an honest and simple age-appropriate explanation (avoiding words with double meanings and false reassurance), and perform the painful procedure in the treatment room so the child's bed and the playroom stay safe. Comfort measures include a comfort position (upright in the parent's lap or chest-to-chest rather than lying flat and being held down), a topical numbing cream before the needlestick when time allows, and age-appropriate distraction such as bubbles, a video, or guided breathing. For infants, nonnutritive sucking, swaddling, oral sucrose, facilitated tucking, skin-to-skin, or breastfeeding help. Therapeutic play lets the child handle equipment and gain a sense of control. Afterward the nurse comforts and praises the child, offers a comfort item, and never threatens or shames.
providerOrders: Keep the parent present to comfort the child; encourage participation
Prepare the child honestly with simple, age-appropriate language
Perform painful procedures in the treatment room, not the bed or playroom
Use comfort positioning, topical anesthetic, and age-appropriate distraction
Comfort and praise the child afterward; provide a comfort item and rest

opts.text: Atraumatic care aims to minimize the child's physical and emotional distress
Keeping the parent present helps reduce the child's separation anxiety
Honest, simple, age-appropriate explanation prepares the child
Painful procedures should be done in the treatment room, not the child's bed
Telling the child a painful procedure 'won't hurt at all' builds trust
Comfort positioning and distraction reduce fear and pain

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What guides comfort during the procedure?

opts.text: A comfort hold (upright, parent's lap) is preferred over lying flat and restraining
Topical anesthetic before a needlestick reduces pain when time allows
Distraction is matched to the child's developmental stage
For infants, pacifier, swaddling, and oral sucrose provide comfort
The playroom is a good place to perform blood draws
Therapeutic play lets the child handle equipment and gain understanding

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order preparing the child for the IV start

opts.text: Encourage the parent to stay and participate as a comforter
Give an honest, simple, age-appropriate explanation
Move to the treatment room, keeping the bed and playroom safe
Apply topical anesthetic and set up distraction
Use a comfort hold during the procedure, then praise and comfort

steps.sub: Does the action support atraumatic care?

Explain the procedure honestly in simple words
Apply numbing cream before starting the IV
Use bubbles or a video to distract the child

Do the blood draw in the playroom
Threaten the child to make them hold still

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Offer a comfort position (parent's lap, upright) rather than lying flat
Start age-appropriate distraction (bubbles, video, breathing)
Perform the procedure efficiently with the parent present
Comfort and praise the child immediately afterward
Provide a comfort item and let the child rest or play it out
